project__lead_agency_information.lead_agency: City of Riverbend Community Development Department

project_location.cross_streets: East Willow Avenue and South Riverbend Parkway
project_location.within_2_miles_schools: Riverbend High School, Willow Creek Elementary School, Sierra Community College – Riverbend Center

development_type.recreational_description: Approximately 12.3 acres of public and private recreational amenities including a central 6-acre community park with multi-use sports field, children’s playgrounds, picnic areas, and a small performance lawn; a 2.5-acre riverfront greenway with a paved multi-use trail, river overlook, and non-motorized boat launch; two pocket parks totaling 1.1 acres integrated into the residential neighborhoods; and on-site indoor fitness and community rooms (about 10,000 square feet) associated with the mixed-use buildings for use by residents and office tenants.
development_type.transportation_type: Construction of internal public streets (approximately 1.8 linear miles), Class II and Class IV bicycle facilities, enhanced pedestrian crossings, a new signalized intersection at East Willow Avenue and South Riverbend Parkway, and a new transit stop with bus pull-out and shelter on East Willow Avenue in coordination with Riverbend Transit.
development_type.other_development_description: Public infrastructure improvements including stormwater detention and water quality treatment basins; undergrounding of existing overhead utility lines along portions of East Willow Avenue; and dedication of a 3.0-acre riverfront conservation easement for habitat protection and passive recreation.

project_issues_discussed_in_document.project_issues_other: Energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions associated with construction and long-term operation of the mixed-use village, including building energy efficiency, transportation-related emissions, and solid waste generation.

land_use__project_description.present_land_use_zoning_general_plan_des: The approximately 78.4-acre project site is currently designated "Business Park" and "Urban Reserve" in the City of Riverbend 2035 General Plan and is zoned BP (Business Park) and UR (Urban Reserve). The site has historically been used for low-intensity agricultural purposes (primarily seasonal grazing and hay production) and contains several older accessory agricultural structures in poor condition. Surrounding land uses include single-family residential neighborhoods to the north across East Willow Avenue, the Feather River and associated riparian corridor to the south, a community college satellite campus and sports fields to the east, and a small industrial/warehouse area and the Union Pacific Railroad main line to the west.
land_use__project_description.project_description:
The Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project proposes to transform an underutilized 78.4-acre infill site along the Feather River into a compact, walkable, mixed-use neighborhood that combines housing, neighborhood-serving commercial uses, office employment, and substantial public open space.

Residential Development: The project includes 325 residential units in a variety of housing types intended to serve a mix of households, including young professionals, families with children, and downsizing seniors. Approximately 210 units would be attached townhomes and small-lot single-family homes ranging from 1,400 to 2,100 square feet, with 2–4 bedrooms. An additional 115 units would be provided in three- to four-story mixed-use buildings above ground-floor commercial space, primarily studio, one-bedroom, and two-bedroom apartments. A minimum of 10 percent of the total units (approximately 33 units) would be deed-restricted as affordable to moderate-income households consistent with the City’s inclusionary housing ordinance. The residential neighborhoods are organized around a central community park and an interconnected network of pedestrian paseos and bikeways.

Commercial and Office Development: The project proposes approximately 85,000 square feet of neighborhood-serving commercial space and 60,000 square feet of office space. Commercial uses are anticipated to include a small-format grocery store (approximately 25,000 square feet), cafes and restaurants, a neighborhood pharmacy, personal services (such as a dry cleaner and hair salon), and small professional offices. The 60,000 square feet of office space would be located in two three-story multi-tenant buildings oriented along South Riverbend Parkway and designed to accommodate professional, medical, and creative office tenants. At full buildout, the commercial and office components are expected to support approximately 430 permanent jobs, ranging from part-time retail positions to full-time professional and administrative roles.

Parks, Open Space, and Recreation: Approximately 12.3 acres of the site would be dedicated to parks, open space, and recreational amenities. A central 6-acre community park would include a multi-use turf field lined for youth soccer, a half-court basketball area, two children’s playgrounds serving different age groups, shaded picnic areas with barbecues, a small performance lawn suitable for community events such as outdoor movie nights or farmer’s markets, and restroom and storage facilities. A 2.5-acre riverfront greenway would be preserved and enhanced along the Feather River, incorporating a paved multi-use trail, native riparian plantings, interpretive signage about the river ecosystem and local history, and a non-motorized boat launch for kayaks and paddleboards. Two pocket parks, totaling about 1.1 acres, would be integrated into the residential neighborhoods, providing small gathering spaces, community gardens, and seating areas. In addition, the mixed-use buildings would include approximately 10,000 square feet of indoor common amenity space, such as fitness rooms, co-working areas, and community meeting rooms available to residents and office tenants.

Transportation and Circulation Improvements: The project would construct a complete internal street network consisting of a primary east-west main street, two north-south neighborhood streets, and several local residential streets, totaling approximately 1.8 linear miles of new public roadways. Streets would be designed as "complete streets" with sidewalks on both sides, Class II bike lanes on collector streets, and traffic-calming features such as bulb-outs and raised crosswalks near parks and schools. A new signalized intersection would be installed at East Willow Avenue and South Riverbend Parkway to provide safe access to the site and improve traffic operations. The project would also coordinate with Riverbend Transit to construct a new bus stop with a shelter, seating, real-time transit information, and bicycle racks along East Willow Avenue, improving transit access for residents and employees. Off-street parking would be provided in a combination of surface lots, on-street spaces, and tuck-under garages, with electric vehicle charging infrastructure installed in accordance with current building code requirements.

Infrastructure and Utilities: The project would extend and upsize existing water, sewer, and recycled water lines from adjacent streets into the site in coordination with the City of Riverbend Utilities Department. Stormwater would be managed through a network of landscaped bioretention planters, underground detention facilities, and two centrally located stormwater basins designed to capture and treat runoff before discharge to Willow Creek and the Feather River, consistent with the City’s stormwater quality standards and regional NPDES permit. Existing overhead utility lines along a portion of East Willow Avenue would be undergrounded as part of the project, improving both aesthetics and service reliability. All new development would be constructed to meet or exceed the 2022 California Green Building Standards Code (CALGreen), with high-efficiency fixtures, solar-ready roofs, and a strong emphasis on pedestrian and bicycle connectivity to reduce vehicle miles traveled.

Phasing and Construction: Buildout of the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village is anticipated to occur in three primary phases over approximately 6 to 8 years, depending on market conditions. Initial phases would focus on backbone infrastructure, the central community park, and the first residential neighborhoods, followed by delivery of the mixed-use commercial center and office buildings. Construction activities would generally occur between 7:00 a.m. and 7:00 p.m. on weekdays, with limited weekend work as needed, in compliance with the City’s noise ordinance. A detailed construction management plan would be implemented to minimize temporary impacts to nearby residents, including designated haul routes, dust control measures, and coordination with Riverbend Unified School District to avoid conflicts with school drop-off and pick-up times.

Overall, the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project is intended to implement the City’s long-term vision for a compact, mixed-use riverfront neighborhood that provides much-needed housing, local-serving retail, and employment opportunities while enhancing public access to the Feather River and protecting sensitive riparian habitat through careful site planning, habitat restoration, and a permanent conservation easement along the riverfront.
